title: Kubernetes 101 — Codespaces & DevContainer Workflow
description: Test your knowledge of the GitHub Codespaces development workflow used in this enablement: devcontainer lifecycle and port management.
imagine: You are a Dynatrace Solutions Engineer building your first enablement lab using the Codespaces framework. You have to make decisions about the devcontainer lifecycle and port visibility.
yourGoal: Answer two questions that cover the operational workflow of building and running enablements inside GitHub Codespaces with the Dynatrace Codespaces Framework.

story.introduction: The Dynatrace Codespaces Framework automates Kubernetes cluster setup, app deployment, and documentation publishing. Understanding its lifecycle is key to building reliable labs.
story.context: Answers can be found in the lab pages covering Getting Started, Codespaces, and Content.

questions.title: DevContainer lifecycle hooks
questions.content: The framework uses `post-create.sh` and `post-start.sh` scripts. When does `post-create.sh` execute?

options.text: Every time the Codespace is resumed from a stopped state
options.explanation: That's when post-start.sh runs. post-create runs only once.

options.text: Only once, when the container is first created (not on subsequent starts)
options.explanation: Correct! postCreateCommand runs exactly once after the initial container build.

options.text: Manually, when the user types 'post-create' in the terminal
options.explanation: It's triggered automatically by the devcontainer lifecycle, not invoked manually.

options.text: During the Docker image build phase, before the container starts
options.explanation: That would be a Dockerfile RUN step. postCreateCommand runs after the container is running.

questions.explanation: The devcontainer lifecycle has distinct hooks: postCreateCommand (once on creation), postStartCommand (on each start), and postAttachCommand (on each VS Code attach).
hints: Think about what 'create' means in the Codespace lifecycle vs 'start'.
post-create is for one-time setup (install tools, deploy apps); post-start is for recurring tasks.

questions.title: Making Codespace ports public
questions.content: The lab says ports are exposed locally by default. How do you make an exposed port publicly accessible from outside the Codespace?

options.text: Add a firewall rule in the devcontainer.json file
options.explanation: devcontainer.json can declare forwarded ports but doesn't manage public visibility.

options.text: In VS Code's Ports tab, right-click the port and change visibility to 'Public'
options.explanation: Correct! GitHub Codespaces controls port visibility through the VS Code Ports panel.
kubectl expose creates a Kubernetes Service — it doesn't control Codespace port visibility.

options.text: Push the code to a public repository
options.explanation: Repository visibility and port visibility are completely independent.

questions.explanation: GitHub Codespaces ports default to 'private' (only the Codespace owner can access). Changing to 'public' in the Ports tab makes the forwarded URL accessible to anyone with the link.
hints: The lab page mentions 'go to the ports section in VSCode, right click on them'.
This is a Codespaces platform feature, not a Kubernetes feature.

learningObjectives: Distinguish between devcontainer lifecycle hooks (post-create vs post-start)
Control port visibility in GitHub Codespaces
